cards.grammarNote: ال (al-) = 'the.' It attaches directly to the noun with no space. كتاب (kitab) = a book; الكتاب (al-kitab) = the book. Arabic has no word for 'a/an' — an indefinite noun has no article.
Sun letter: ط (ta') causes the 'l' in ال to assimilate. الطعام is written al-ta'am but pronounced at-ta'am. The spelling never changes — only the pronunciation.
Sun letter: د (dal) assimilates the 'l' of ال. Written al-dajaj, pronounced ad-dajaj.
Classic example of a sun letter: ش (shin) assimilates the 'l.' Written ال + شمس but pronounced ash-shams, not al-shams. The sun letters include: ت ث د ذ ر ز س ش ص ض ط ظ ل ن.
No article = indefinite: قهوة (qahwa) = a coffee. Compare: القهوة (al-qahwa) = the coffee. The difference between 'a coffee' and 'the coffee' is simply the presence or absence of ال.
Arabic uses ال for generic categories: أحب الخبز (I like bread/the bread = bread in general). Unlike English which uses no article for generics, Arabic often uses ال.
When an adjective modifies a definite noun, the adjective also takes ال: الخبز (def.) + الطازج (def.) = 'the fresh bread.' Compare: خبز طازج (indefinite) = 'fresh bread.'
Moon letters (حروف قمرية) do NOT assimilate the 'l' of ال. م (mim) is a moon letter, so المستشفى is pronounced al-mustashfa (with clear 'l'). Moon letters: أ ب ج ح خ ع غ ف ق ك م ه و ي.
